title: Strengthening VA Patient Advocacy for Rural Veterans Act of 2025
short_title: Strengthening VA Patient Advocacy for Rural Veterans Act of 2025
plain_summary: The Strengthening VA Patient Advocacy for Rural Veterans Act of 2025 helps veterans who live in rural areas get better support when they need medical care. The bill requires VA hospitals to have special people called coordinators who will help rural veterans when they receive care away from the main hospital. It also makes sure patient advocates answer directly to the hospital directors so they can do their jobs better. Finally, the VA must write reports every year to show how well they are helping veterans with their medical problems and concerns.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Veterans' Affairs.

sections.title: Section 1. Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Strengthening VA Patient Advocacy for Rural Veterans Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2. Improvement of Office of Patient Advocacy of Department of Veterans Affairs
sections.text: This section changes an existing law about patient advocates at the Department of Veterans Affairs. It makes several important updates to help veterans get better care and support. The changes include creating special coordinators for rural veterans, improving how patient advocates report to their supervisors, and requiring regular reports about how well the patient advocacy system is working. These changes are made by adding new parts to section seven thousand three hundred nine A of title thirty eight of the United States Code. Part f creates coordination of care in rural and highly rural areas. The director of each VA medical center must choose at least one patient advocate to serve as a coordinator for veterans living in rural and highly rural areas. This coordinator helps veterans who get care at community based outpatient clinics or through the community care network instead of at the main medical center. The person chosen for this job must do all the regular patient advocate work but focus specifically on helping rural veterans who get care outside the main medical center. Whenever possible, the medical center director should choose someone who already works at the medical center for this job. Part g requires that medical center directors be included in the chain of reporting. The Director must make sure that every patient advocate at a VA medical center reports to that medical center's director at some point in their reporting structure. Part h creates an annual report requirement about information in the patient advocate tracking system. At least once every year, the Secretary must send a report to the Senate Committee on Veterans Affairs, the House Committee on Veterans Affairs, and the Director of each Veterans Integrated Service Network. This report must explain information collected through the Patient Advocate Tracking System or whatever system replaces it. Each report must include information that does not identify specific people. The report must cover common problems that were reported, how long it took to solve those problems, how long it took to answer requests for information, and any compliments or complaints received through the system.
